required_sections.notes: Sections 3 (Learning Objectives ~80w), 4 (Introduction ~130w), 5 (Core Concepts ~1430w across 6 subsections), and 10 (Key Takeaways ~140w) all present and non-trivial. All meet or exceed minimum word thresholds.

citation_density.notes: All 5 primary resources cited at least once: [1] python.org tutorial, [2] stdtypes reference, [3] realpython variables, [4] realpython data types, [5] w3schools. Virtually every factual claim in sections 5, 6, and 7 carries at least one citation marker. Estimated claim-citation rate ~85%, well above the 60% threshold. No fabricated markers detected — all [N] values resolve to entries in topic.resources.json.

sequence_drift.notes: print() is used in code examples but is explicitly named-and-deferred with a dedicated Note block at the start of section 4 pointing to topic 11.4. Arithmetic operators and conditionals are named only with explicit deferral pointers (topics 11.4 and 11.6). call_ai_model() is labeled as hypothetical. type conversion functions (int(), str()), string methods, f-strings, lists, and dicts are absent. No concept is taught beyond the topic's own introduced set.

length_sanity.notes: Estimated ~2500 prose words (excluding code block bodies). Within the task-specified standard profile range of 1500-3500 words. Note: SKILL.md defines standard as 3000-4400w; at ~2500w this is slightly below that floor but within the task prompt's stated range. Flagged as a soft observation only.

topic_scope.notes: All coverage stays within the headline 'Variables — naming and storing values'. In-scope concepts (variable, =, str/int/float/bool, naming rules, type(), snake_case, reassignment, dynamically typed) are taught. Out-of-scope concepts (print, arithmetic, conditionals, type conversion) are only named with explicit deferral pointers, never taught. No scope creep detected.

beginner_readability.notes: All technical terms (variable, assignment operator, data type, str, int, float, bool, type(), snake_case, reassignment, dynamically typed) are defined at first use with plain-language explanations and concrete everyday analogies (sticky notes, light switch, counting eggs, measurements). No paragraph introduces more than one new term at a time. Sentence lengths are short and conversational. No jargon dumps, no academic hedging phrases detected.

verdict: All 6 checks passed — corpus is well-cited, on-scope, sequentially clean, beginner-readable, and within length range; print() is properly named-and-deferred with a note block.
